turns.text: Hello Diedre, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're in the middle of your pregnancy, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

*shifts in seat, pulls cardigan a little tighter*

Yeah, okay. I appreciate you asking. Honestly, it's... it's a lot right now, but I'm willing to talk about it.

*pauses, choosing words carefully*

So this is my seventh pregnancy, and you'd think it would feel routine by now, right? But it doesn't. Every time I walk into that clinic, I feel like I'm holding my breath. The staff are fine—they're not *unkind* or anything—but I'm always aware that I'm taking up space there. That there are probably people who think I shouldn't be.

The medical side is what keeps me up at night, though. I read too much, I think. I know enough to be dangerous—I understand the statistics, the risk percentages, all of it. So when they tell me I'm "low risk," part of me wants to believe that. But another part of me is... *searching*, you know? Looking for what they might have missed. My last ultrasound, the tech got really quiet for a moment, and I just about lost it internally.

*voice gets quieter*

It's not just about the baby's health, either. I'm scared of complications that might come with *attention*. I need this pregnancy to go smoothly and quietly. I need to be the kind of patient nobody has questions about.

Is that—does that make sense?
Tell me about the moment you found out you were pregnant — and what the weeks that followed were like for you.

*takes a breath, eyes getting a bit distant*

Okay, so... I found out at home. One of those dollar store tests because I couldn't afford anything else that week. I remember staring at those two pink lines for maybe five minutes straight, just sitting there on my bathroom floor. And honestly? My first thought wasn't joy or panic—it was *calculation*. How far along am I? What does that mean for my job situation? How am I going to afford this?

*rubs face*

The weeks after were surreal. I told the father, and... he's not in the picture anymore. So it's me, my six other kids—they're older now, teenagers mostly—and this new baby coming. My kids, they were excited. That was the one pure thing about it, you know? My youngest hugged me when she found out.

But me? I went into overdrive. Started reading everything. Medical journals, blogs, forums at two in the morning. I'd lie awake thinking about Down syndrome, about neural tube defects, about all the things that can go wrong. *Especially* things that might not show up on the standard screening. 

*looks down at hands*

The clinic visits started feeling heavy pretty quick. I'd prepare questions but then swallow them because I didn't want to seem like a difficult patient. I have enough complications already without adding that label. So I smile, I nod, I say I'm fine... and then I go home and spiral a little.

Does that answer your question?
Tell me about your antenatal care — the appointments, check-ups, and the people who looked after you during your pregnancy. What was that experience like overall?

*shifts uncomfortably, choosing words carefully*

The appointments themselves are... fine. The clinic is clean, the staff shows up on time mostly. My doctor, Dr. Martinez, she seems competent. She knows her stuff. But there's this thing that happens—I feel like I have to perform being the "good patient." Grateful, compliant, not asking too many questions.

*voice becomes more hesitant*

Like, at my last visit, my blood work came back clear, and the technician just said "Everything looks good" and moved on. But I wanted to ask about the specific values, the false negative rates for the screening tests, especially given my age and parity. Instead, I just... nodded. Because if I start asking detailed questions, start seeming like I'm *worried* or *difficult*, what does that signal? Does it flag me somehow?

*fidgets with sleeve*

The worst part is the silence. You know? When the ultrasound tech goes quiet, or when someone looks at my chart and their expression shifts slightly. I can't tell if I'm reading into it or if there's actually something there. And I can't just ask directly because—

*pauses, looking troubled*

—because asking too many questions, drawing too much attention to myself, it's risky. For me specifically. I have a lot riding on staying under the radar here. So I get the care, technically, but it doesn't feel like anyone's really *seeing* me. Not as a person with legitimate concerns. Just as another statistic in their system.

It's lonely, honestly.
Tell me about any screening or testing you were offered or had during your pregnancy — how did you make sense of it all and make decisions about it?

*leans forward, speaking more intensely*

Oh man, the screening stuff. That's where my brain goes into overdrive, you know?

So they offered me the standard first trimester screening—the combined test with the nuchal translucency ultrasound and the blood work. I did it because, well, you're supposed to, right? But I spent weeks beforehand reading about sensitivity rates, specificity, what the actual risk numbers mean versus what doctors *present* them as.

*rubs temples*

When the results came back "low risk," everyone expected me to relax. My doctor said the numbers were reassuring. But I kept thinking about the studies I'd read about false negatives, about how "low risk" for a 31-year-old isn't the same as "no risk." And with my history—seven pregnancies, six healthy kids—I know statistically I'm doing well. But that doesn't stop the what-ifs.

*voice gets quieter, more conflicted*

They mentioned the cell-free DNA testing, the NIPT, as an option but made it sound optional. I wanted to do it because I wanted *more* information, more reassurance. But it costs money I don't have right now, and also... I hesitated. Because more testing means more data in my medical record. And I don't know if that's paranoid thinking or just realistic caution given my situation.

*looks directly at you*

I made decisions based on what felt safe—medically and otherwise. But "safe" for me means two different things, and they're not always compatible.
How was the information about tests explained to you — did you feel you had enough to make the decision that was right for you?

*exhales slowly, leaning back*

Honestly? It was... surface level. Dr. Martinez explained the screening in maybe five minutes. She said, "We recommend this test. It measures markers for chromosomal abnormalities. Your age puts you at low risk." Then she handed me a pamphlet and asked if I had questions.

*voice becomes tinged with frustration*

I didn't ask any. Because in that moment, I could feel her moving through her checklist, getting ready to move on to the next patient. And I'm sitting there thinking about false positive rates, about what happens if the screening comes back abnormal—like, would that trigger more invasive testing? Would that create a paper trail? But I just took the pamphlet and nodded.

*picks at cuticle*

The pamphlet was... generic. Numbers presented in a way that made everything sound very controlled and reassuring. But it didn't explain the nuance. Like, what does "low risk" actually *mean* numerically? What are my specific percentages based on my age and history? What would a positive result entail? What are my options then?

*looks frustrated but resigned*

I think the information was there, technically. But not in a way that made me feel equipped to actually *decide*. It felt more like informed consent as a checkbox—"Did we tell her? Yes. Does she understand? Probably doesn't matter."

*voice drops*

And maybe that's fine for women who don't spend their nights researching epidemiology. But for me? I wanted dialogue, not a script. I wanted to feel like my concerns were valid, not... paranoid.
Tell me about where you turned for information and support during your pregnancy — what sources mattered most to you, and why?

*laughs a little, but it's tinged with exhaustion*

Oh God, the internet. That's my therapist, my obstetrician, and my support group all rolled into one at two in the morning when I can't sleep.

*settles back, more animated*

I read everything. Medical journals when I can access them, PubMed articles, research on maternal age and genetic risk. I'm on a few pregnancy forums—anonymous ones, obviously—where I can ask questions I'd never ask my actual doctor. Like, "Is it normal to be this anxious about screening results?" or "What are the real statistics on undetected trisomies?" There's something about the anonymity that lets me be honest.

*voice becomes quieter, more vulnerable*

But it's a double-edged thing. The more I read, the more I realize how much I don't know. And how easy it is to catastrophize. I'll read one study about advanced maternal age and suddenly I'm convinced something's wrong. Then I read a different study and feel momentarily reassured. It's exhausting.

*fidgets with hands*

What I really *needed* was someone to talk to face-to-face. A counselor, maybe, or even just a nurse who had time to sit with me and explain things without rushing. Someone I could trust to listen without judgment. But that's not really available at the clinic, and... 

*hesitates*

...and even if it were, I'm not sure I'd feel safe opening up about all my fears. There's too much at stake for me to be fully vulnerable with the system.

So the internet it is. It's imperfect, but it's *there*.
Did you use any apps, websites, forums, or social media groups? What did you get from those that you couldn't get elsewhere?

*nods, warming up a bit to this question*

Yeah, I'm on a few apps. There's one pregnancy tracking app I use—it tells me what size the baby is that week, what's developing. That's comforting in a weird way, seeing the milestones listed out. Makes it feel real and also... manageable? Like there's a timeline and things are happening as they should.

*leans in slightly*

But the forums are where I actually *talk*. There's this one specific subreddit for older mothers—well, "older" is their word, not mine—and women there get it. They understand the anxiety without you having to explain it away. Someone will post about worrying over screening results, and instead of "Oh, you're low risk, stop worrying," people say, "Yeah, I obsessed over this too. Here's what I found helpful." It's validating.

*voice becomes more guarded*

I'm also in a private Facebook group for women navigating pregnancy without a partner. That's... different. More practical. People share resources, childcare tips, how to handle questions from family. Less medical anxiety, more survival tips.

*pauses, choosing words carefully*

What I *don't* find anywhere is someone who understands my specific situation—the immigration piece, the fear of drawing attention. I can't really post about that stuff. So I read a lot and post very little. I absorb information and stay quiet.

*looks at you directly*

The apps and forums gave me knowledge and community, which felt like power. But they also fed the anxiety sometimes. More information doesn't always mean more peace.
Tell me about how your body changing during pregnancy felt for you — physically and emotionally. How did you relate to those changes?

*shifts uncomfortably, wrapping arms around self*

Physically, it's... familiar. I've done this six times before. The nausea in the mornings, the fatigue, the way my body just does what it needs to do. By the second trimester, that settles down and I start to show more. I can feel the baby moving now, which is—

*softens slightly*

—which is actually the one thing that quiets my mind sometimes. When I feel that flutter, that rolling sensation, it's undeniable proof that something's happening in there. It's real. Not just a statistic.

*voice becomes heavier*

But emotionally? It's complicated. I'm thirty-one, I've had six healthy pregnancies, and I know my body works. That should feel empowering, right? Instead, it feels like pressure. Like I'm supposed to just... breeze through this. No complaints, no anxiety, just accept that my body knows what to do.

*looks down*

The thing is, I'm also terrified of my body. What if this time is different? What if carrying this pregnancy while everything else in my life is so precarious—the job situation, the uncertainty, the stress—what if that affects the baby? I catch myself checking in obsessively. Am I eating enough? Is the baby moving enough? Is my stress literally damaging my baby?

*voice drops*

And there's this weird shame in it too. Like, I should be grateful to have a body that can carry children. But instead I'm hyperaware of every symptom, every change, wondering what it means. My body doesn't feel like mine anymore. It feels like a vessel I'm responsible for protecting, and I'm terrified I'm failing.
Tell me about the role your partner, family, or close friends played during your pregnancy. What was their involvement like?

*long pause, jaw tightening slightly*

The father... he's not really in the picture. We weren't together when I got pregnant, and once I told him, he made it clear he wasn't interested in being involved. So that's just... the reality I'm working with.

*shifts weight*

My kids are my actual support system. My oldest is eighteen, and she's been incredible—asking how I'm feeling, helping with the younger ones. My teenagers, they're excited about the baby in their own way. They don't treat me like I'm fragile or broken for being pregnant and single again. That means more than I can say.

*voice becomes more guarded*

But I don't talk to them about my fears. The medical stuff, the anxiety about screening results, all of that—I keep it from them. They have enough to worry about. And I'm their mother, you know? I'm supposed to be the stable one, the one holding things together. So I perform that for them.

*rubs face*

As for friends... I have people I know, but I wouldn't call them close. I work, I take care of my kids, and there's not much room for deep friendships. And honestly, my immigration situation makes me cautious about who I let into my inner world. You don't know who might judge you or, worse, report something.

*looks directly at you, vulnerability showing through*

So I'm doing this mostly alone. My kids are here, but the real fear, the real anxiety? That stays inside my head at night. There's no one I can fully confide in about how terrified I am—not just about the baby, but about everything.
How included did your partner or support person feel in appointments and decisions — and how did you feel about that?

*lets out a short, bitter laugh*

There is no partner at appointments. That's the thing—it's just me and whoever's at the clinic that day. My oldest came to one appointment early on, just to have someone there. But I could tell she felt out of place, like she didn't know what questions to ask or how to help. So I told her she didn't need to come back. I didn't want her to feel burdened.

*fidgets with sleeve*

Which means all the decisions fall on me. All of them. The screening tests, the timing of appointments, what information to ask for or not ask for. And that's... it's a lot of weight to carry alone when you're already anxious.

*voice becomes quieter*

I think the clinic staff probably assumes there's a partner somewhere, or at least a support person. They ask things like "Have you discussed this with your partner?" or "Will someone be with you at delivery?" And I just say no, and I can see them mentally checking a box. *Single mother. Low social support.* I can feel it in how they adjust their tone sometimes, like they're speaking to someone more fragile.

*looks frustrated*

But honestly? I'm not sure I'd want a partner at these appointments anyway. It's easier to be guarded when it's just me. No one to worry about, no one to perform stability for in front of medical staff. Just me making quiet decisions and moving forward.

*pauses*

Though I won't lie—it would be nice to have someone who actually *understood* what I'm going through.
Tell me about any worries or fears you carried during your pregnancy — about your health, your baby, the birth, or anything else.

*takes a deep breath, eyes getting shiny*

Oh God. Where do I even start?

*voice becomes quieter, more intense*

The medical stuff is constant. I lie awake thinking about chromosomal abnormalities, about things that might not show up on ultrasound until it's too late. I know the statistics—I *know* I'm low risk—but that doesn't stop my brain from spinning through worst-case scenarios. What if the screening missed something? What if there's a developmental delay they haven't caught? I read about cases where babies seemed fine on all the tests and then... they weren't.

*rubs eyes*

And then there's the fear that's less about medicine and more about my life. I'm not in stable employment right now. Finding consistent work has been brutal. How am I going to afford another baby? How am I going to take time off for recovery? My older kids are teenagers—they need things too. I'm terrified of failing them because I'm stretched so thin.

*voice drops to almost a whisper*

But the biggest fear, the one I can barely articulate... it's about my immigration status. If something goes wrong during pregnancy or birth—if there are complications that require extended hospital stays, if there are questions about my documentation—what happens then? Could a medical issue become a legal issue? Could my baby be taken from me? 

*looks up, eyes wet*

I carry that fear every single day. It's not rational maybe, but it's real. And I can't talk to my doctor about it because admitting it feels dangerous. So I just... hold it inside and hope nothing goes wrong.
Were you able to share those fears with your care team — and if so, how did they respond?

*shakes head slowly, almost immediately*

No. I didn't share them. Not really.

*wraps arms tighter around self*

I mean, I mentioned to Dr. Martinez that I was feeling anxious about the screening results, and she basically said, "Your numbers are good, try not to worry." Like anxiety is just something you can turn off with reassurance. She didn't ask *why* I was anxious or what specifically was driving it. Just... reassurance and move on.

*voice becomes more strained*

The immigration stuff? God, no. I would never tell them that. I don't know if they're mandated reporters or what their obligations are. I don't know if admitting I'm undocumented would somehow trigger something in their system. So that stays completely locked down.

*looks directly at you, pain evident*

The employment stuff I mentioned once—I said finding work had been challenging. And the nurse kind of patted my arm and said, "We have resources," but then never followed up. No one actually asked what I needed or how I was managing financially.

*voice becomes bitter*

I think they see "single mother, low risk, shows up to appointments" and think everything's fine. They don't dig deeper. And honestly? I'm relieved they don't because I'm not sure I could be honest if they did. The risk of being honest feels bigger than the relief of being heard.

*pauses, looking exhausted*

So I smile, I say I'm managing, and I go home and fall apart in private. That's the deal I've made.

When you first started thinking about having a child, what did you hope your relationship with your healthcare providers would look like — and were there any ways you worried it might not live up to that?

*goes quiet for a long moment, staring at hands*

Honestly? I didn't really think about it that much before this pregnancy. With my other six, I just... went to appointments, got checked out, and that was it. It was transactional. I wasn't expecting much, so I wasn't disappointed.

*voice becomes more reflective*

But this time, maybe because I'm older or because I've read so much, I found myself wanting something different. I wanted someone—a doctor, a nurse, *someone*—who would actually see me. Not just as a case number or a statistic. Someone who would listen to my concerns without making me feel like I'm being paranoid or difficult. Someone who would explain things in a way that respected my intelligence instead of talking down to me.

*looks up, vulnerability showing*

I wanted to feel like a partner in my own care, you know? Not just a body carrying a baby that they're monitoring. I wanted someone I could trust enough to be honest with, to ask the hard questions without fearing the consequences.

*voice cracks slightly*

And yeah, I was worried from the start that wouldn't happen. Because I know how I'm perceived. I'm a single mother of six, I'm struggling with employment, I have an accent that doesn't quite fit anywhere. I'm not the ideal patient. So I braced myself for indifference, for being rushed through, for not being truly heard.

*pauses*

And that's pretty much what I got. So at least I wasn't surprised by the disappointment.

Thinking back to when you and your partner — or the people closest to you — were weighing up whether and when to start trying for a baby, how did those conversations actually go? Who felt like they had the most say, and did that feel right to you?

*shifts uncomfortably, lets out a short laugh*

There wasn't really... a conversation, to be honest. Not the kind you're describing.

*looks away*

I wasn't actively "trying" for this baby. It happened. I was with someone, things weren't protected properly—whether that was negligence or just the reality of life moving fast, I'm not entirely sure anymore. When I realized I was pregnant, I told him. He made it very clear he didn't want to be involved. And that was that.

*voice becomes quieter, more reflective*

So in terms of who had the "say"... I guess I did, by default. Because once he stepped away, it was my decision. Keep the baby or don't. And I chose to keep her because—

*pauses, considering*

—because by the time I knew for sure, she was already real to me. And I've never regretted having my other children, even when things were hard. I know I can do this.

*looks at you directly, something protective in her expression*

But "chosen" isn't quite the right word either. It wasn't like I sat down and made a deliberate plan. It was more like... this is what happened, and now this is what I'm doing. There wasn't a romantic narrative about it. Just survival and responsibility.

*voice becomes softer*

Sometimes I wonder what it would've been like to have that kind of conversation—where someone actually wanted to plan a baby *with* me. But that's not my life. My life is making the best of what comes.
Did older family members — parents or grandparents — have a view that found its way into the decision, even indirectly?

*pauses, expression becoming guarded*

My mother... she passed away several years ago. So she's not here for any of this, which is hard. I think she would've been supportive, though. She had me young, and she understood what it meant to be a mother without all the stability you're supposed to have.

*fidgets with hands*

My father's still around, but we're not close. He was never really involved with my other pregnancies either. He has his own life, his own family situation. I don't really factor into his calculations, and honestly, that's fine. I've never expected him to.

*voice becomes more strained*

The thing with family is... a lot of my extended family is in the same situation as me. Immigration, precarious work, all of it. So there's this unspoken understanding that you don't judge each other too harshly for how you're surviving. But there's also this underlying stress, you know? Like everyone's trying to hold it together and not burden each other with their own problems.

*looks down*

No one ever said "You shouldn't have another baby" or "This is a bad idea." But I also felt the weight of not wanting to ask for help, not wanting to be a drain on people who are already struggling themselves. So I made my decision quietly, and I didn't really... consult anyone.

*voice softens*

Sometimes I wonder if I would've made a different choice if I had someone older I could actually talk to about it. But I didn't, so this is where I am.
Before you became pregnant, how did you feel about your body and what it was capable of — and did those feelings change at all as you started thinking about pregnancy?

*takes a moment, choosing words carefully*

Before this pregnancy? I felt... accomplished, actually. My body has done something remarkable six times over. I've carried babies, birthed them, recovered. That's not nothing. There's a certain strength in that, even if nobody really acknowledges it.

*voice becomes more complex*

But there's also this thing where, as a woman without a stable partner, your fertility becomes... complicated. Like, society's message is "You should have wanted this differently. With the right person. In the right circumstances." So even though my body worked beautifully, the *context* of what I was doing with it felt shameful sometimes. Six children, no husband. People made assumptions.

*rubs arms*

I think I made peace with my body's capability despite the judgment. I trusted it. I knew what it could do.

*voice shifts, becomes more anxious*

But this pregnancy? This seventh one? It's different. I'm older now—not old, but older. And suddenly I'm hyperaware of aging, of decline, of things that might go wrong that didn't before. I read about maternal age and risk factors, and my body that once felt capable now feels like it's on borrowed time.

*looks troubled*

I'm still proud of what my body can do. But now I'm also afraid of it. Afraid it might fail me, or worse, fail my baby. The confidence got tangled up with anxiety somewhere along the way. And I can't quite untangle it.

If you saw a GP, nurse, or any other health professional before you became pregnant — even just for something routine — what was that experience like, and did it give you any sense of what maternity care might be like down the line?

*shakes head*

I didn't really see anyone before this pregnancy. That's the thing—when you're managing on your own, bouncing between jobs, trying to keep your head above water, preventive care isn't really on the radar. I go to the clinic when something's wrong or when I'm pregnant. That's about it.

*voice becomes more matter-of-fact*

I've had routine appointments over the years for my other pregnancies, obviously. And a few times I've gone in for things like a persistent cough or whatever. But those experiences were... fine? Efficient. In and out. No real relationship building. The staff does their job, asks their questions, and moves on.

*leans forward slightly*

What I noticed, though, is that nobody ever treated me like my health mattered beyond the immediate problem. Like, if I came in sick, they'd treat the sickness. But they wouldn't ask about nutrition, about stress, about whether I had stable housing or insurance. Just... the symptom, the treatment, goodbye.

*voice becomes quieter*

So when I started maternity care, I kind of expected the same thing. And that's what I got. They're monitoring the pregnancy itself—the baby's development, my vital signs—but they're not really *seeing* me as a person with a full life that's affecting this pregnancy.

*looks at you*

I guess what I'm saying is, my previous healthcare experiences primed me not to expect much. So maternity care being impersonal felt... normal. Predictable. Which is its own kind of sad, isn't it?
Did you see the same person more than once, or were you always meeting someone new?

*nods slowly*

Dr. Martinez is my main doctor. I see her at most appointments, which is... good, I think? At least there's some continuity. She knows my history—all seven pregnancies, my previous deliveries. She doesn't have to start from scratch every time.

*pauses thoughtfully*

But the ultrasound technicians are different people almost every time. And sometimes if Dr. Martinez is running behind, I'll see a nurse practitioner instead. So it's not like I have one consistent person who knows me deeply.

*voice becomes more nuanced*

Honestly, I'm not sure if continuity would even help in my case. Because seeing the same person means they might start asking more probing questions. They might notice patterns in my anxiety or my guardedness and want to dig deeper. And I'm not sure I want that level of scrutiny, even from someone familiar.

*shifts in seat*

With Dr. Martinez, we have a routine. She's professional, competent. But we don't have a *relationship*, you know? I don't feel like I can be fully myself with her. So in a weird way, having different people sometimes feels safer. Less chance of someone really getting to know me well enough to see through the performance.

*looks conflicted*

Part of me wishes there was someone I'd built actual rapport with. Someone who'd check in on me beyond the clinical stuff. But another part of me is relieved there isn't. It's easier to keep my guard up when nobody's getting too close.
Before you were pregnant, what kinds of conversations were you having with the people around you — family, friends, your partner — about having a baby, and whose input felt most meaningful to you?

*lets out a long breath, looking almost trapped by the question*

Honestly? I wasn't really having those conversations. That's the hard truth of it.

*voice becomes quieter*

With the father, there was no real conversation about babies or the future. We were just... together in the moment. When it became clear I was pregnant, that's when we had to talk, and by then it was too late for discussion about *whether*. It was about *what now*.

*picks at cuticle*

My kids knew I was seeing someone, but I didn't sit down and say, "Hey, how would you feel if I had another baby?" They're teenagers—they have their own lives. And honestly, I didn't want to put that burden on them, to make them feel responsible for my reproductive decisions.

*voice becomes strained*

As for friends or extended family... I don't have deep enough relationships where I'm having those kinds of conversations. Most people in my life are dealing with their own survival stuff. Nobody's really asking, "So, Diedre, are you thinking about having more kids?" Because that feels like a luxury topic when you're all just trying to get through the week.

*looks at you, pain evident*

I think that's part of what makes this so isolating. I didn't have a community weighing in on this decision because I don't really have that kind of community. It was just... me, alone, making a choice that nobody else had much stake in.

*pauses*

Maybe if I had those conversations, things would feel different now.
Did your mum, mother-in-law, or older women in your family share their own experiences — and did that help or complicate things?

*face softens, becomes more wistful*

My mom... I wish she was here for this. She had me when she was young, unmarried, and she just... did it. She worked, she raised me, she didn't apologize for it. I think she would've understood what I'm going through in a way nobody else can.

*voice becomes quieter, sadder*

But she's been gone for several years now. So I don't get to ask her questions about pregnancy, about managing alone, about how she dealt with the judgment. I carry her example with me—this sense that you just keep moving forward—but I can't actually talk to her about it.

*shifts uncomfortably*

As for my partner's mother... there's no real relationship there. He stepped away, so his family wasn't part of this either. And honestly, I wouldn't have felt comfortable reaching out to them anyway. That would've felt like overstepping.

*looks thoughtful, a bit sad*

The older women in my extended family, they've had their own struggles—pregnancy, work, immigration stuff. But we don't really share those stories in a deep way. It's more surface level, you know? "Oh, pregnancy was hard" or "Yeah, I worked through mine too." But not the real vulnerability underneath.

*voice becomes reflective*

I think if my mom was here, I'd feel less alone in this. Less like I'm the only one navigating all of this with no roadmap. Her presence alone would've meant something.

Were there things about your own body, your health history, or just the way you felt about yourself physically that made the idea of pregnancy feel complicated or uncertain — even before anything had actually happened?

*takes a long time to answer, looking inward*

Yeah. There were things.

*voice becomes hesitant*

I've had six pregnancies, six deliveries. My body's been through a lot. And while I'm proud of what it's accomplished, there's also this... wear and tear, I guess? I'm thirty-one, but sometimes I feel older. The fatigue isn't just from life circumstances—it's physical exhaustion that doesn't fully go away between pregnancies.

*rubs lower back absently*

And there's always this undercurrent of worry about complications. Not because I've had serious ones, but because I *know* statistically they can happen. Gestational diabetes, preeclampsia, placental issues—I've read about all of it. Every pregnancy after thirty carries slightly more risk, and I know that intellectually.

*voice becomes more vulnerable*

But there's also this deeper thing I don't really talk about. Being a single mother, my body feels like it's not just mine. It's this... vessel for other people's judgments. When I'm pregnant, people look at me differently. They make assumptions. And I internalize that—like my body is somehow irresponsible for getting pregnant again without a partner.

*looks down*

So there's this complicated shame mixed with capability. My body works beautifully, but the *context* of what I'm doing with it feels wrong to the outside world. And I've absorbed some of that feeling, even though I know logically it's not fair.

*pauses*

I think that uncertainty was always there, beneath the surface. This pregnancy just made it louder.
Did you have any concerns about your weight, fitness, or a health condition that felt like it might be a barrier?

*pauses, considering the question carefully*

Not really in the way you're asking. My weight's been stable, I'm not significantly overweight or underweight. I'm managing physically—working when I can find work, keeping up with my kids, all of that.

*shifts slightly*

But the fitness thing... I don't have time or money for a gym. I walk a lot because I don't always have reliable transportation. That's my exercise, I guess. And honestly, after six pregnancies and the life I'm living, I'm not thinking about "getting fit for pregnancy." My body either can do this or it can't, and based on history, it can.

*voice becomes more thoughtful*

The health condition piece is more about stress and exhaustion than anything physical. I have chronic anxiety—has for years—but I don't really have it formally diagnosed or treated because, well, healthcare access isn't straightforward for me. So that's more of a mental health barrier than physical.

*looks slightly uncomfortable*

What I *do* think about is how my body is perceived. If I gained weight during pregnancy, would staff judge me more harshly? Would they assume I'm not taking care of myself? I'm hyperaware of being seen as a "bad" patient—someone who doesn't deserve as much care or attention because of my circumstances.

*voice quiets*

So it's less about actual health conditions and more about fear of judgment based on how my body looks and what my life looks like. That feels like the real barrier.

In the time before you became pregnant, was there any one person — professional or otherwise — who felt like a consistent, reliable source of support or guidance, someone you could come back to with questions?

*goes very quiet, then shakes head slowly*

No. There really wasn't.

*voice becomes small*

I mean, my oldest daughter—she's been there in practical ways. Helping with her siblings, asking how I'm feeling. But she's eighteen. I can't burden her with being my support system. That's not her job.

*looks away*

Professionally? No. The doctors I've seen over the years have been fine, but they're not people I could "come back to" with questions. They're transactional. You show up, they address the immediate issue, you leave. There's no relationship there that would make me feel comfortable calling them up with a worry or asking follow-up questions.

*voice becomes more strained*

And that's partly by design, honestly. I don't *want* to build those kinds of relationships with authority figures because I'm afraid of what they might do with the information I give them. A consistent relationship means someone gets to know you deeply, and deep knowledge can be dangerous when your immigration status is precarious.

*pauses, looking conflicted*

But it's also lonely. Really lonely. I handle things alone because I have to, and because I don't know who to trust. So I read articles at night, I spiral on forums, I carry my fears in silence.

*meets your eyes*

There should be someone. Someone reliable, consistent, who actually knows me. But I don't have that. And I'm not sure I'd know how to accept it even if I did.

As you were thinking about whether to have a baby, did it feel like a decision you were genuinely free to make in your own time and your own way — or were there pressures, expectations, or circumstances that meant it was never quite fully your choice?

*sits back, expression becoming complicated and pained*

That's... a really good question.

*takes a long pause*

Honest answer? It didn't feel like a choice at all. Not in the way you're describing. There was no moment where I sat down and thought, "Do I want another baby?" It just... happened. And once it happened, the only real choice was whether to continue or not. And by that point, she was already real to me.

*voice becomes quieter*

But underneath that, there are all these pressures I can barely articulate. There's the pressure of being a woman whose body *can* have babies, so maybe I should be grateful for that capability. There's the pressure of being a single mother—like, what's one more when you're already doing it alone? There's the pressure of my circumstances being what they are, so planning and control feel like luxuries I don't get to have.

*looks troubled*

And then there's this deeper thing. With my immigration status, my job instability, everything being so precarious... sometimes I wonder if I was ever truly *free* to make reproductive decisions at all. Like, did I choose this baby, or did my circumstances just... make this the path of least resistance?

*voice cracks slightly*

If I had stable employment, safe housing, legal status, a partner who wanted to be involved—would I have made a different choice? I honestly don't know. And that uncertainty is part of what makes this so heavy.

*pauses*

I don't think I was free. Not really.
Were there financial, relationship, or cultural factors that made you feel like the timing was out of your hands?

*nods slowly, voice becoming heavy*

All of it. All three.

*leans forward*

Financially, I've never been in a position where I could say, "Now is a good time to have a baby." There's never been a good time. I work when I can find work—it's inconsistent, it's precarious. Some months are better than others, but there's always this baseline of struggling. So the idea of *planning* a pregnancy around financial stability? That feels like a fantasy I can't afford.

*voice becomes more strained*

The relationship piece is obvious—there wasn't one. Not a real partnership anyway. So that decision was made for me the moment he stepped away. I either had this baby alone or I didn't.

*rubs face*

And culturally... this is harder to explain. In my community, there's this understanding that you have children. It's what women do. You might do it in less-than-ideal circumstances, but you do it. My mother had me young and alone. My extended family has seen single mothers, teenage mothers, mothers without resources. It's normalized in a way that's both liberating and constraining.

*voice becomes quieter*

Like, nobody's shocked that I'm having a seventh baby without a partner. But there's also this underlying message that this is just what happens to women like us. That we don't really get to plan or control our fertility the way more privileged women do. We just... survive what comes.

*looks at you directly*

So was the timing in my hands? No. Not really. I was managing circumstances, not making choices.
Once you were pregnant, how much did you feel like you were in the driving seat of your own care — and were there moments when you felt yourself handing that over to others, willingly or not?

*laughs bitterly*

In the driving seat? I don't think I ever was, honestly.

*settles back, voice becoming more reflective*

From the moment I told the clinic I was pregnant, it became about *their* protocols, *their* timelines, *their* decisions about what testing I should have and when. I show up to appointments—that's the extent of my agency. I'm cooperative, I follow instructions, I don't push back.

*picks at hands nervously*

And yeah, some of that is willingly handing it over. Like, I trust that Dr. Martinez knows medicine better than I do. I'm not a doctor. So when she says I need bloodwork or an ultrasound, I do it. That feels reasonable.

*voice becomes more conflicted*

But there's another layer where I'm handing it over not because it's medically necessary, but because I'm afraid. I'm afraid to ask too many questions because that might flag me as difficult or paranoid. I'm afraid to insist on certain tests or information because that draws attention. So I'm compliant in a way that's not really *choosing* to trust my providers—it's more like... capitulating.

*looks troubled*

And then there's the part I haven't really handed over but desperately want to. I want someone else to carry some of this weight. To tell me what to do, to reassure me, to actually *see* me and my situation. But I can't ask for that because asking means being vulnerable in ways that feel dangerous.

*voice drops*

So I'm driving and not driving at the same time. It's exhausting.

When decisions came up during your pregnancy — about tests, appointments, birth plans, or anything else — how did you and your partner navigate those together, and did it ever feel like you weren't quite on the same page?

*shifts uncomfortably, voice becoming flat*

There is no partner to navigate with. That's the reality I'm living.

*pauses, then continues more quietly*

So when decisions came up, it was just... me. Alone. Making choices about my body and my baby with nobody else's input or perspective.

*looks away*

And maybe that sounds freeing in theory, but it's actually isolating. Like, when I was deciding about the screening tests, I had no one to talk it through with. No one to say, "Hey, do you think we should do the NIPT?" or "I'm worried about this, what do you think?" I just sat with my anxiety and my research and made a decision in silence.

*voice becomes more strained*

The birth plan thing—I haven't even really thought about it deeply because there's no one to plan *with*. It's just going to be me, laboring, with whoever happens to be at the hospital. No partner to advocate for me, to hold my hand, to help me communicate what I need.

*rubs arms*

And honestly? I think that's why I'm so anxious about everything. Because there's no shared decision-making, no one to ground me when I'm spiraling. It's all on me to figure out, all on me to carry, all on me to live with the consequences.

*looks at you directly, vulnerability showing*

I didn't get to have that partnership experience. So yeah, we're not on the same page. There is no "we."

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

*pauses, taking a moment to gather thoughts*

Yeah, actually. There is something.

*voice becomes more deliberate*

I want people to understand that being "low risk" doesn't mean you're not scared. And being anxious doesn't mean you're ungrateful for your pregnancy or your body's capability. These things can exist at the same time, and that's real.

*leans forward slightly*

But more than that... I need people to understand that maternity care isn't just about medicine. It's not just about checking boxes—bloodwork, ultrasounds, vital signs. It's about whether a woman feels *seen* and *safe*. And I don't feel either of those things, even though Dr. Martinez is technically competent.

*voice becomes more passionate*

When you're already navigating so much—precarious employment, immigration fears, doing this alone—you need healthcare providers who can hold some of that weight with you. Not judge you for it, not make you feel like a liability, but actually *acknowledge* that you're managing a lot and that matters.

*pauses, voice quieting*

And maybe this is selfish, but I also want someone to ask me how I'm really doing. Not the clinical "How are you feeling?" but actual concern. Because I'm not fine. I'm managing, but I'm not fine. And there's a difference.

*looks directly at you*

I hope this helps. I hope something I've said makes a difference in how care gets provided to women like me.
